questions.text: Which equipment set is the core of what a Type II certification allows you to service?

options.t: High- and very-high-pressure appliances over 5 lbs — residential split systems, heat pumps, and most commercial DX/refrigeration
Only factory-sealed units charged with 5 lbs or less
options.why: That describes Type I small appliances. Type II is the high-pressure, over-5-lb category (split systems, commercial DX).

options.t: Only low-pressure centrifugal chillers
options.why: Low-pressure chillers are Type III. Type II is high/very-high pressure equipment.

options.t: Only motor-vehicle air conditioning
options.why: MVAC is Section 609, a separate certification. Type II covers stationary high-pressure appliances.

questions.why: Type II covers high- and very-high-pressure appliances containing more than 5 lbs (or requiring charging on install): residential/light-commercial split systems, heat pumps, supermarket racks, most commercial DX — the everyday HVAC tech certification.
questions.ref: EPA 608 Type II definitions; 40 CFR 82 Subpart F

questions.text: You are recovering from a very-high-pressure R-410A split system (under 200 lbs) with a recovery machine built in 2019. What evacuation level does the EPA table require?

options.t: 0 in Hg (atmospheric) — small very-high-pressure appliances under 200 lbs recover to 0 in Hg with post-1993 equipment
10 in Hg, the same as a large R-22 system
options.why: 10 in Hg applies to appliances of 200 lbs or more of that class. Under 200 lbs recovers to 0 in Hg with post-1993 equipment.

options.t: 500 microns
options.why: Microns are the dehydration target before recharging, not the EPA recovery level (which is stated in inches Hg).

options.t: 15 in Hg
options.why: 15 in Hg is a deeper table row for other classes/sizes. A sub-200-lb very-high-pressure system with post-1993 equipment is 0 in Hg.

questions.why: Per the EPA required-evacuation table, very-high-pressure appliances under 200 lbs recover to 0 in Hg (atmospheric) with recovery equipment manufactured after Nov 15 1993. The deeper levels apply to ≥200 lbs and to other pressure classes.

questions.text: A 150-lb commercial comfort-cooling system exceeds its leak-rate trigger. Under the refrigerant-management rules, the leak must generally be repaired within:

options.t: 30 days (with defined extensions for parts availability, retrofit or mothballing)
12 months from the date the leak was noticed
options.why: The general repair window is 30 days once the trigger is exceeded, not a year.

options.t: There is no time limit as long as refrigerant is added when needed
options.why: "Just keep topping it off" is exactly the practice the leak-repair time frame exists to stop.

options.t: 90 days in all cases with no possibility of extension
options.why: The baseline is 30 days; specific, documented extensions exist (parts, retrofit, industrial mothball), not a flat 90-day rule.

questions.why: Once an appliance above the charge threshold exceeds its annual leak-rate trigger, leaks must generally be repaired within 30 days, followed by verification tests — with defined extensions for parts, retrofit or mothballing.
questions.ref: EPA leak-repair requirements (40 CFR 82 Subpart F / ERR rule)

questions.text: Before calculating a leak rate, what must you establish first?

options.t: The appliance's full charge, from nameplate/OEM data, measurement, or an established range
The refrigerant's GWP, since the leak rate is weighted by climate impact
options.why: Leak rate is a percentage of full charge, not GWP-weighted. You need the full charge as the denominator.

options.t: Nothing — leak rate is just the pounds added, regardless of system size
options.why: Pounds added alone is not a leak rate; it must be expressed as a percentage of the full charge, so the full charge must be known.

options.t: The ambient temperature at the time of service
options.why: Ambient conditions matter for charging accuracy, not for defining the leak-rate denominator, which is full charge.

questions.why: Leak rate is computed as a percentage of the appliance's full charge, so you must first establish full charge from nameplate/OEM data, measurement, or an established range — getting the denominator right makes the trigger meaningful.
questions.ref: EPA leak-rate calculation methods (40 CFR 82 Subpart F)

questions.text: How do "high-pressure" and "very-high-pressure" appliance classes map to common refrigerants for Type II purposes?

options.t: R-22 and R-134a are high-pressure; R-410A is very-high-pressure — both are Type II
R-410A is low-pressure and therefore Type III
options.why: R-410A is very-high-pressure (Type II). Low-pressure (Type III) means refrigerants like R-123/R-11.

options.t: R-22 is low-pressure and handled under Type III
options.why: R-22 is high-pressure (Type II). Low-pressure refers to R-123/R-11-class refrigerants.

options.t: Pressure class is irrelevant to which certification type applies
options.why: Pressure class is exactly what separates Type II (high/very-high) from Type III (low) equipment.

questions.why: EPA groups appliances by refrigerant saturation pressure: very-high-pressure (R-410A) and high-pressure (R-22, R-134a) fall under Type II; low-pressure (R-123, R-11) falls under Type III. The class also drives recovery targets.

questions.text: While evacuating a split system for a compressor change, you reach a deep vacuum and want to confirm the system is dry before recharging. Which target applies to that goal?

options.t: 500 microns — an industry dehydration/evacuation target, verified by holding vacuum with a micron gauge
10 in Hg, the EPA recovery number, since it is the deepest requirement
options.why: The EPA in-Hg recovery levels are about how much refrigerant to remove, not about dryness before recharge. Dehydration is measured in microns.

options.t: 0 in Hg is sufficient to confirm the system is dry
options.why: Atmospheric pressure is not a dehydration standard; confirming dryness uses a deep micron-level vacuum (commonly 500 microns) held stable.

options.t: There is no way to confirm dryness; you simply recharge
options.why: Dryness is confirmed with a micron gauge and a decay/hold test at a deep vacuum, commonly targeting 500 microns.

questions.why: Confirming a system is dry before recharge uses a deep micron-level vacuum (commonly 500 microns) held stable on a micron gauge — an industry dehydration practice, distinct from the EPA in-Hg recovery levels that govern how much refrigerant to remove.
questions.ref: ACR evacuation/dehydration practice; distinct from 40 CFR 82.156 recovery table
